title: Unit 7 Checkpoint Quiz — Preterite vs. Imperfect

questions.prompt: Which tense sets the 'background scene' in a past story?
options: imperfect
preterite
present
future
questions.explanation: The imperfect describes ongoing states, background conditions, and habitual actions — it sets the stage. The preterite delivers the plot events.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct tenses: ___ (leer) cuando ___ (sonar) el timbre.
options: Leía / sonó
Leí / sonaba
Leía / sonaba
Leí / sonó
questions.explanation: Ongoing background action (reading) = imperfect: leía. Interrupting event (doorbell rang) = preterite: sonó.

questions.prompt: What does 'Conocí a mi esposa en una fiesta' mean?
options: I met my wife (for the first time) at a party.
I used to know my wife at a party.
My wife and I went to a party.
I was at a party with my wife.
questions.explanation: 'Conocer' in the preterite means to meet someone for the first time. This is a completed, specific event — the moment of first meeting.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses BOTH tenses?
options: Eran las tres cuando llegó mi papá.
Fueron las tres cuando llegaba mi papá.
Eran las tres cuando llegaba mi papá.
Fueron las tres cuando llegó mi papá.
questions.explanation: Time (eran) = imperfect. The arrival (llegó) = preterite, specific completed event. This is the natural combination.

questions.prompt: 'De niño siempre comía en casa de mi abuela.' What does this sentence describe?
options: A habitual past action (imperfect)
A specific event that happened once (preterite)
A future plan
A current habit
questions.explanation: 'Siempre' + childhood context + imperfect ('comía') = habitual past action. This describes something that happened regularly, not a one-time event.

questions.prompt: What does 'No pude abrir la puerta' suggest, versus 'No podía abrir la puerta'?
options: No pude = I failed to open it (specific attempt); No podía = I was unable to open it (ongoing state)
No pude = I used to not open it; No podía = I failed to open it once
Both mean exactly the same thing
No pude = I didn't want to open it; No podía = I couldn't open it
questions.explanation: 'No pude' (preterite) = I tried and failed on a specific occasion. 'No podía' (imperfect) = I was generally unable to, an ongoing condition.

options: De repente + imperfect (describing a sudden event)
Mientras + imperfect (describing background action)
Ayer + preterite (specific past event)
Siempre + imperfect (habitual past)
questions.explanation: 'De repente' (suddenly) signals an abrupt, completed event — this calls for the PRETERITE, not the imperfect. Using imperfect with 'de repente' would be a tense mismatch.

questions.prompt: In Mexican oral storytelling, what does the shift from 'estaba yo...' to '...y entonces llegó...' signal?
options: The shift from background setup (imperfect) to main event (preterite)
A change of subject
The end of the story
A shift from present to future
questions.explanation: 'Estaba yo' (imperfect) opens the scene. 'Y entonces llegó' (preterite) launches the main action. This is the classic two-tense storytelling rhythm in Mexican Spanish.
